data.title: Nebius: Did Meta And Microsoft Buy Compute At Near-Zero Margin? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last year, I upgraded Nebius (NBIS) to a "Buy" after its first (and transformative) deals with Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT).

However, looking at NBIS's profit margins after the initial racks came online, I am starting to question how these contracts were priced. Meta's capacity under the terms of the deal has been fully deployed and online as of January 2026. The first tranche of MSFT's racks became online last November, with most of the remaining capacity coming online in the second half of 2026.

There are early signs that NBIS might have been accepting extremely low (or even unprofitable) margins on its raw-compute contracts with hyperscalers in order to scale and pursue its dream of becoming a full-stack cloud provider itself.

The first step of pursuing this dream is building the physical infrastructure. The coming paragraphs examine the motives of hyperscalers supporting a potential competitor.

I am downgrading Nebius to a "Hold" until more clarity on the unit economics of its raw compute deals with Meta and Microsoft is revealed in the coming quarters.

Growth

Nebius' fast rise and recent US expansion have attracted attention from investors. The company reported FY'25 growth of 479% YoY, with revenue reaching $530 million just ~18 months after inception in mid 2024.

Demand for GPUs from hyperscalers is a key driver for this growth. NBIS gets a solid allocation of Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs. During the Q4'25 earnings call last February, management highlighted that its GPU base is in the hundreds of thousands, and while hyperscalers have more GPUs, the count is still measured in the hundreds of thousands as well, putting NBIS in the same class as hyperscalers. Meta and Microsoft signed deals worth billions. NBIS expects to end 2026 with an Annual Revenue Run Rate "ARR" between $6 to $9 billion.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates NBIS a solid A on growth, with working capital being a key drag against a perfect A+ rating. Demand for compute is high but costly to build. NBIS says it is particularly focused on maintaining both a solid balance sheet and avoiding dilution, leaning on customers for prepayments in addition to revenue to fund growth. These dynamics do impact working capital growth, as mentioned above, but simultaneously set NBIS apart from other compute providers such as IREN (IREN) and Applied Digital (APLD), but at par with CoreWeave (CRWV).

If my hypothesis is true, we should see an increasing divergence in the balance sheets and unit economics of raw-compute-only data center REIT providers on one hand and neo-clouds with full-stack aspirations on the other.

Balance Sheet

NBIS is an infrastructure operator with a balance sheet of a tech company with just ~$500 million in net debt. For a company running $5.5 billion in Properties, Plants and Equipment "PPE" and putting the groundwork for a further $16 billion to $20 billion of additional CapEx, that's a modest debt.

Management says that it plans to use more debt to fund its CapEx. That's a good corporate finance practice. But, overall, the debt ratio is modest, with only 62% of assets funded via debt. Equally important, NBIS says that equity raises will continue to be limited.

One way management funds its infrastructure buildout is by asking for pre-payments from its customers. Deferred revenue stood at $1.6 billion as of December 2025.

Management delivered all the capacity under its contract with Meta. In 2026, it will be installing GPU racks under the Microsoft contract. In total, NBIS expects CapEx between $16 billion and $20 billion ($18 billion at midpoint), with management noting that 60% of funding secured via multiple arrangements and balance sheet accounts.

Margins

NBIS's Q4'25 margins tie all the pieces of the investment thesis together, highlighting the risks and opportunities. Simply put, NBIS's AI infrastructure business isn't profitable. The risk is that management doesn't have plans to become profitable in the short term. This is serious because I am talking about profitability on a contract-level, not because of temporary items related to growth or revenue ramp. This could be more fundamental.

The math of infrastructure projects is simple. The lifespan of a GPU across the industry is 4-6 years. (Note: Starting January 2026, NBIS will assume five years for depreciation, up from four years). As more power-efficient chips come to market, it becomes less logical to use older, more power-hungry chips, given the power shortage and running costs. A typical 8 GPU computing node, such as DGX B200, sells for roughly half a million dollars. Excluding installation, cables, cooling, interconnects, etc, the breakeven contract price of this node should be $100,000 per year for the GPU hardware ($500,000 over a 5-year lifespan).

The question now is, why is NBIS unprofitable? Management's own adjusted net income, designed to reflect the net income run rate by excluding non-recurring gains/expenses, stood at a negative $173 million in Q4'25.

Note: Some analysts highlight that NBIS is profitable on a cash basis, ignoring depreciation, the largest and only link between CapEx and returns. That's an erroneous analysis. The lifespan assumption could also be used to attack the unprofitability thesis. If there is a discrepancy between the actual life of the chips and their accounting lifespan used to calculate depreciation, NBIS GAAP accounting might underestimate actual profits, and cash basis accounting becomes important. We examine this scenario in more detail in the risk section.

What's Nebius's End Game?

If NBIS's contracts aren't profitable, then why do they do what they do? I believe that NBIS is playing the long game here. They want to become a public cloud hyperscaler (like Google Cloud, AWS, and Azure). However, the market is difficult to penetrate. To become a hyperscaler, you need data centers across the world. That would be expensive. Thus, they offer hyperscalers an attractive price. While the contract pricing doesn't cover the entire infrastructure buildout, it does cover most of it. Hyperscalers, on the other hand, are willing to support a potential competitor for two reasons. First, there is a shortage in GPUs, and NVDA is supporting neo-Clouds such as NBIS by allocating GPUs in response to hyperscalers attacking its own turf by developing their own chips (AMZN runs AI compute on Trainium and Inferentia, while Meta and MSFT have MITA and Maia and other proprietary microchip programs. Secondly, the price discount offered by NBIS is hard to ignore.

Risks

One might argue that the GPU lifespan is more than five years, citing the recent reports that demand for H100 has pushed compute prices on these devices up. NBIS notes that capacity for its older chips is sold out, and unlike what analysts expected, compute prices have remained firm. That might be true, but these are exceptional circumstances because of the computer shortage. Users are using what they've got. However, I can imagine some applications that are okay with a lower token per second per watt if the price is right.

Secondly, a long-term upside risk is that NBIS might eventually succeed in becoming a public cloud provider, at least important enough to have a meaningful market share in this expansive, high-margin business.

Final Thoughts

A rating downgrade is warranted after NBIS reported persistent and deep losses in the quarters subsequent to the ramp-up in sales on the back of its deals with Meta and MSFT. There is a chance that this hypothesis is wrong, and that profitability might be due to a ramp in revenue (think overhead costs tied to construction and deal arrangements). However, typically, when this is the case, management makes it perfectly clear on earnings calls. This didn't happen in Nebius's case. Instead, management emphasized how non-core its deals with Meta and MSFT are. I will not wait and speculate on unit economics. Until things become clearer in the coming quarters, I am downgrading NBIS to "Hold".

data.title: Stellantis: Alliance With Chinese Automaker Leapmotor Points To A Long, Tough Climb
data.description:
Stellantis N.V., following a dreadful 2025 that included a $26.3 billion annual net loss, faces a long, steep and difficult recovery to remain viable in the face of rising competition from the Chinese automotive industry and uncertainty over regulation governing electrification in major markets.

The automaker’s guidance for 2026, issued in late February, is relatively vague:

“a mid-single-digit percent increase in net revenues, a low-single-digit AOI margin, and improved industrial free cash flow generation year over year. Sequential improvement is also expected from the first half to the second half of the year.”

An early green shoot – not yet indicative of too much – is Stellantis’ 4% first-quarter increase in its U.S. unit sales against the backdrop of a domestic industry that reported an approximate 6% market decline. Following a business reset in the U.S. with new leadership and a pivot favoring the company’s strongest nameplates, sales were boosted by the Ram pickup truck and the Jeep division.

Production bump

On April 15, Stellantis announced a 12% YOY global shipment increase of vehicles to dealers, a total of about 1.36 million units. Shipments were up significantly across all major regions. In the U.S., the increase was driven by demand for Ram 1500 Hemi V8, refreshed Jeep Grand Wagoneer and the all-new Jeep Cherokee, Stellantis said.

General Motors Company (GM) and Ford Motor Company (F), which had invested heavily into battery electric vehicles (BEVs), both saw significant unit declines for the quarter in the U.S., evidence that Stellantis has the ability to claw back lost market share against its domestic rivals.

Stellantis had been developing BEVs at a slower pace than its U.S. rivals. Following the exit of previous CEO Carlos Tavares and promotion of Antonio Filosa to the post, the automaker has adjusted its mix of propulsion offerings, emphasizing some powerful gasoline engines like the Hemi and accelerating development of extended range electrified vehicles (EREVs).

Historically known as a “muscle car” automaker in the U.S., Filosa appears bent on restoring that reputation.

Ram 1500 REV is an EREV that offers 169 miles of range using battery only and 690 miles of range total when the on-board engine with a full tank of gasoline recharges the battery. EREV technology may provide a transition from gasoline-only engines to a future in which battery-only powertrains are seen by consumers as more practical alternatives.

Production is scheduled to begin for the Ram EREV model as well as a Jeep version later this year. The technology will be unfamiliar for most U.S. consumers; its reception by the market could be consequential for STLA’s financial performance.

European production, which accounts for about 48% of Stellantis’ total, was up 12% in the first quarter. The increase was driven by models based on Stellantis’ Smart Car platform, Fiat Grande Panda and the Opel/Vauxhall Frontera. According to Automotive News, Stellantis has been cutting European prices aggressively to reduce inventory, especially of slow-selling models. Peugeot e-208 small BEV hatchback is available to lease for 150 Euros a month, no money down.

At the annual meeting on April 14, Filosa told shareholders the company is taking “painful but necessary steps” to build market share, revenue and margin. Unrealistic optimism for BEV sales were a factor behind write downs underlying last year’s immense financial losses.

The latest “momentum is real,” Filosa said. Financial reporting on a quarterly basis, in contrast to the current semiannual basis, will begin April 30. Filosa is scheduled to present the company’s strategic plan in detail at an Investors Day on May 21.

Help from the east

Collaboration with the Chinese auto industry is taking shape as a key tenet of the company’s long-term strategy. Stellantis is in early talks with China’s Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Co. (ZJLMF) – of which it owns a bit less than 20% -- to build an Opel-branded SUV, probably in Spain. The targeted output at Stellantis’s Zaragoza plant would be about 50,000 units, starting in 2028.

The talks also include the possibility of an Alfa Romeo BEV model at Zaragoza.

The collaborative approach now looks highly rational and likely what was envisioned when STLA spend 1.5 billion Euros to buy a 20% Leapmotor stake in 2023. At the time GM and Ford were investing multiple billions to forge their own BEV strategy and infrastructure; Stellantis lacked similar financial resources. In retrospect Stellantis was fortunate because much of what it might have invested probably would have produced very little in the way of profitable sales.

Ford has been the most open and candid about the extent to which Chinese automakers and battery manufacturers now lead the global automotive industry, in terms of performance and technology.

North American landing?

Stellantis and Leapmotor already have their eye on North America following Canada’s decision in January to lower the import tariff to 6.1% (compared to the U.S.’s more than 100% tariff) on BEVs imported from China. Stellantis owns an empty assembly plant in Brampton, Ontario that might be a local assembly site for Stellantis/Leapmotor BEVS if objections from Canadian politicians can be overcome.

Consumers are gradually migrating to BEVs in Europe and the North America, Stellantis’s two most important markets. The tricky part for management will be maintaining sufficient sales and profit growth from traditional models over the next decade or two to fund electrification. Even so, finances will be tight: new internal combustion design and digital tech will consume capital on top of the resources required for new BEV models.

The competitiveness of BEV models based on Leapmotor design and technology hasn’t been tested widely in Western markets against BYD (BYDDF), Geely (GELYF) and other large Chinese enterprises. In China, Leapmotor is known as a “value leader” for its aggressive low pricing; at the same time, it lacks the cachet of better known Chinese brands.

Many question marks remain for Stellantis and its Chinese partner, though the company is pointed in the right direction and showing early – very early – signs of recovery. I’ll stay with my Hold recommendation on STLA.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SGOV: Cash Is Not Trash (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
This year is shaping up to be one of the more exciting years in post-pandemic investing. While 2025 saw tariff madness and trade wars, 2026 appears to be hosting a more kinetic type of conflict. Most recently, President Trump has ordered a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most significant acts of economic disruption in modern naval history. Trump’s daring use of warfare to reshape the global energy economy has generated volatility far away from where the missiles are landing.

Some folks are undoubtedly looking at current events with a desire to sit this one out. Things are looking a little spicier than my taste buds have a liking for, and more of my assets are moving towards cash and cash alternatives. Among the traditional options are high-yield savings accounts and money market mutual funds, both of which offer unique forms of principal protection while also offering a relatively high yield.

However, what these two options lack is liquidity. A high-yield savings account doesn’t generally offer the ability to invest, and a money market mutual fund trades just like that—a mutual fund. Another option one may consider is an exchange-traded fund, specifically the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV). This article will follow up on my prior coverage and explain why SGOV remains a top cash alternative.

Review Of Prior Coverage

I have covered SGOV twice on Seeking Alpha, most recently in November 2024. This article included a relatively comprehensive overview of the portfolio, expense structure, and distribution setup of the fund.

SGOV is an exchange traded fund sponsored by BlackRock (BLK), one of the world’s largest asset managers. The fund is part of the iShares core lineup, which is designed to track indices and charge rock bottom expense ratio. Across the lineup, each fund charges a competitive fee. In the case of SGOV, this fee is just nine basis points, cheaper than most money market funds. SGOV buys U.S. treasuries that mature within a period of just ninety days. This means a portfolio that is constantly rolling over into three month treasuries. Like other funds in the iShares lineup, the fund tracks an index, specifically, the ICE 0-3 Month US Treasury Securities Index.

The article focused on a discussion on changing interest rates. Specifically, we talked about the diminishing appeal of the fund after benchmark interest rates had peaked. At this point, the Federal Reserve had begun to lower the federal funds rate, and I predicted that the distribution would likely begin to follow.

Ok, I admit, it did not exactly take Sherlock Holmes to see that one coming.

In the article, we discussed changes in the yield curve. This included how interest rates had begun to climb further down the yield curve. This meant that investors had an opportunity to earn greater income while also benefiting from the possibility that interest rates could move lower.

My recommendation was to take a look at alternatives from one of my favorite fixed-income managers, PIMCO. I suggested that funds like the PIMCO Active Bond Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (BOND) might be an attractive alternative for those who were willing to up their risk appetite while avoiding picking individual bonds. Since BOND has done well relative to short-maturity treasuries.

However, we must admit that quite a bit has changed since November 2024.

Cash Isn't Trash

Things continue to change, and it feels like the macroeconomic and geopolitical pictures are changing faster than I can account for. There are a variety of factors that could cause material changes to the economy in the near term. An energy crisis, private credit conundrum, possible AI bubble, and more are coming together to create a storm of uncertainty.

I just published an article detailing some of the risks facing private equity and private credit. Combined with historically high equity valuations and a market that doesn’t seem to know what fear looks like.

The survey showed a net 64% of respondents now consider U.S. stocks overvalued—a reading that, while still elevated, marks a notable retreat from the extreme pessimism that had dominated fund manager outlooks in recent years.

Geopolitical disruptions and risk factors have combined to begin pushing inflation higher once again. The risk of inflation is leading investors to fear that rate cuts may not be as imminent as we had once expected. Looking back, the Fed has been moving the federal funds rate lower until the beginning of 2026.

Looking forward, it appears as though more rate cuts are much less of a certainty than people had previously anticipated.

Benchmark interest rates remaining higher for longer means that the distribution from funds like SGOV remains relatively protected. Again, since the fund invests in short treasuries that mature within three months, the distribution is extremely sensitive to near-term changes in interest rates as the fund is constantly rolling over.

The ability to earn a return commensurate with or above inflation while sitting out some of the larger emerging risks looks like it may not be the worst opportunity in the world.

The Yield Curve Looks Flat

Another option many pursue is moving further down the yield curve to earn a better yield. After all, I made this recommendation in my prior coverage combined with adding an active manager who could invest more flexibly. What looks different today that makes me think SGOV might be a better option once again?

For starters, the yield curve looks very flat.

Treasury yields remain within the federal funds rate range for three years. Moving out to five years, the spread over the upper target of the benchmark interest rate is just 10 basis points. Moving out to the 10-year, the yield is currently just 50 basis points above the upper target of the federal funds rate. To earn a premium of more than 100 basis points, investors need to move out to 20 years, which becomes an entirely different risk assessment.

This means that there is no yield premium to investing in maturities out to three years. Even moving just several years out creates much more interest rate volatility. For example, compare the price of SGOV to the iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY), which invests out to three years.

SHY is considerably more volatile while offering no material return premium. Those who are looking for something that looks and feels more like a money market fund will find SGOV attractive.

Why SGOV?

So, why SGOV and not the myriad other treasury ETFs that are trading? Someone else might ask why not look into one of the newer money market ETFs, but I will save that for another article entirely.

SGOV is one of the largest and most liquid treasury exchange-traded funds, with $25 billion in managed assets and millions of shares of volume each day. This means the fund is large, but not so large that it becomes difficult to manage.

Another consideration is the expense ratio, which is just nine basis points, according to BlackRock, Inc. (BLK).

Conclusion

Right now, cash is looking better and better as uncertainty grows. Those looking for something a little different than a traditional money market fund might consider looking towards something like SGOV with a similarly conservative strategy. Combined with rock-bottom fees and the ability to trade intraday, SGOV earns a Buy rating.

data.title: JFR: Downgrading One Of Our CEF Holdings (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The big story this year has been the Iran war, which has spiked oil prices and brought back the specter of inflation. The rates markets have responded handily, pricing out any Fed cuts this year and moving the entire yield curve higher. This move has helped the battered floating rate leveraged loan asset class, which had seen significant outflows in the past year.

One of our holdings, the Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (JFR), falls into this category. We added this name last year, after a massive evisceration of leveraged loan CEFs when market participants started pricing in Fed cuts:

The trade did really well initially as the market rebounded from oversold conditions but has produced a more neutral result long-term given the actual cuts implemented by the Fed. We can see the vicious rebound after our rating (which marked an interim bottom) and the total return of roughly 7.7% since we assigned the prior Seeking Alpha rating.

In today's article we are going to show readers why we are downgrading the CEF now given peak fear regarding rates. As the Iran war gets solved (one way or another), the market will start repricing Fed cuts, and outflows from leveraged loans will resume. We are now moving the fund to 'Hold' since we do not find the name presenting an attractive entry point anymore, but we will continue to hold it as a building block of our portfolio.

What Does JFR Do? It Holds Leveraged Loans

While investors are reading about the travails of private credit every day, nobody mentions leveraged loans. Private credit and leveraged loans are siblings, with private credit representing direct lending to a client (often floating rate), while leveraged loans are the syndicated version of the same lending action. 'Syndicated' simply means there are several banks that do the lending and then sell the debt in the secondary markets. This translates into better visibility into the respective credits and the ability to trade in and out of them in size, given the liquidity provided by market makers.

JFR invests in leveraged loans:

The Fund seeks to achieve a high level of current income by investing in a portfolio of adjustable rate senior loans and other debt instruments. At least 80% of its managed assets will consist of adjustable rate loans; at least 65% of these must be senior loans secured by specific collateral. Other loans may include unsecured senior loans and secured and unsecured subordinated loans. The Fund uses leverage.

Currently over 83% of the fund holdings are invested in leveraged loans:

The CEF has a classic build when it comes to loans versus bonds, with a small 10% sleeve allocated to fixed-rate bonds.

From a ratings perspective, the fund is overweight in higher-risk single-B names:

Single-B names make up 58% of the holdings, while double-BB loans represent 25% of the portfolio. The fund is granular and diversified, with no issuer representing more than 2% of the overall holdings. Versus simple vanilla leveraged loan ETFs, the fund comes with gearing, which sits at 36%. Leverage turbo-charges returns in good times but can be an accelerator to the downside during recessions.

Leveraged loans are floating rate (meaning they re-set off SOFR, the new Libor), so the fund has a very low duration profile of only 0.5 years. A low duration profile means the sensitivity to interest rates is low, but the fund's credit risk is high given its overweight single-B positioning and leverage on top.

Rates Have Moved Higher This Year

Given the ongoing conflict with Iran, oil prices have spiked, bringing back inflation fears. As a result of potential inflation, rates have shifted higher since the start of the year:

The red line in the graph above is the yield curve at the beginning of the year, while the blue line is the curve as of April 14, 2026. As we can see, the blue line has shifted higher, with intermediate portions of the curve up from 20 to 30 bps (looking at 3- and 5-year maturities). This is the direct result of market participants pricing out Fed cuts this year as a result of potential inflationary pressures.

A move higher in the yield curve and the pricing out of Fed cuts in 2026 have been beneficial for JFR, which has rallied almost +5% off the lows. However, as the ultimate outcome of the war plays out, the market will again move towards cuts and away from floating-rate products. The current rally is just a temporary respite. Longer-term, market participants will favor fixed-rate bonds over floating-rate leveraged loans, as we have seen in flows.

CEF Total Returns

The name has delivered outstanding returns during the high-rate environment but is less appealing when rates are low or heading lower.

We are comparing JFR with the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) and the Eaton Vance Senior Floating Rate Trust (EFR). BKLN is the unleveraged version of JFR and has a different manager as well. EFR is a similar CEF to JFR but from the Eaton Vance team.

We can see the beta that the CEF product adds to total returns: during good times, CEFs outperform a simple vanilla ETF to the upside, while during 'bad' times they outperform to the downside. 'Bad times' does not necessarily equate to a recession but can also equate to a loss of cash flow. We can see how leveraged loan CEFs have outperformed to the downside since mid-2025, when the market started pricing in significant rate cuts. Rate cuts simply mean fewer cash flows to holders, so fixed-rate bonds become more attractive versus leveraged loans.

Discount/Premium To NAV

The CEF is now trading with a high discount to NAV when considered historically:

The loss of appetite for leveraged loans by the market has translated into a discount to NAV for the CEF. While some might say the CEF is now 'cheap,' this is not the case. We can see leveraged loan CEFs trading 'cheap' for long periods of time during low-rate environments. We are of the opinion that this valuation metric now fully reflects the new environment with lower cash flows coming from lower SOFR levels. If we get an actual recession and credit blows up, expect the discount to widen much further. As of now it only reflects low-rate expectations, not a recession.

Distribution Coverage

The fund does a reasonable job of covering its distribution:

JFR comes with an eye-popping 12% distribution rate, but 28% of that is return of capital. Do you expect the CEF to cut its distribution after the Fed cuts two times? We have seen Nuveen draw a line when it comes to ROC utilization above 30% (i.e., it tends not to do that). So if there is less cash flow coming in, the CEF will cut rather than use more ROC. This is fair and healthy for a CEF, because as an investor, you want long-term NAV stability.

Conclusion

JFR is a fixed-income closed-end fund. The name comes from Nuveen and represents a leveraged take on floating-rate loans. The CEF did really well when Fed Funds were very high but has been hit hard by outflows as the market prices in Fed rate cuts. While the CEF's discount is now wide, it is only pricing the current implied Fed cuts (i.e., rates) and not credit issues. A potential recession can further widen out the discount. With Fed cuts set to resume when the outcome of the Iran war is decided, we are no longer sanguine about leveraged takes on floating rate loans like JFR. We are still holding the name as a part of our portfolio but no longer consider the current price as attractive; hence the downgrade from 'Buy' to 'Hold.'

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Europe Stares At Jet Fuel Crunch Amid Hormuz Disruption
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

IEA head warns on largest energy crisis ever. (0:15) Taiwan Semi raises 2026 outlook on strong AI chip demand. (1:50) Microcap stocks surge after adding AI to their names. (2:19)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Europe may have “maybe six weeks or so” of jet fuel remaining, International Energy Agency Executive Director Fatih Birol told the Associated Press, warning of what he called “the largest energy crisis we have ever faced” following the near-shutdown of oil and gas flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

The impact would mean “higher petrol prices, higher gas prices, high electricity prices,” and potential flight cancellations “soon,” Birol said.

The economic strain would hit unevenly, with countries including Japan, Korea, India, China, Pakistan and Bangladesh on the front line, along with poorer nations across Asia, Africa and Latin America. “Then it will come to Europe and the Americas,” he added.

“If the Strait is not reopened,” Birol warned, “soon we will hear the news that some of the flights from city A to city B might be canceled as a result of lack of jet fuel” in Europe.

Meanwhile, a senior Chevron executive told consumers facing high energy prices they may just want to “try to drive less” and turn out the lights.

“We should always be conserving energy, whether it's your light switch or the miles you drive or what kind of car you buy,” Andy Walz, Chevron’s president of downstream, midstream and chemicals, told CBS. “I would encourage everybody to try to conserve, hang in there and hopefully prices will be coming down soon.”

Among active stocks, PepsiCo (PEP) reported improved Q1 results in its convenient foods segment after pledging price reductions in December. The company said its brand refresh efforts, innovation pipeline and affordability initiatives are gaining traction.

For the full year, Pepsi expects organic revenue growth of 2% to 4% and core constant-currency EPS growth of 4% to 6%, with midpoints ahead of consensus.

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) raised its 2026 revenue outlook on strong AI chip demand after Q1 net income surged 58% Y/Y. For Q2, the chipmaker guided to revenue of $39B to $40.2B, implying 10% sequential growth and 32% Y/Y growth at the midpoint.

PPG Industries (PPG) is rallying after announcing price increases of up to 20% across its global paints and coatings portfolio, citing higher raw material, energy, logistics and packaging costs.

In other news of note, echoes of the dot-com bubble and blockchain mania are reverberating in an AI-adjacent corner of the market this week.

Penny stock Myseum (MYSE) surged more than 250% today after the privacy-focused AI and social media technology company rebranded itself as Myseum.AI.

That follows a striking pivot from wool-based shoe company Allbirds (BIRD), which saw its market cap jump roughly $125M after announcing it would rebrand as NewBird AI, raise up to $50M in funding, and “seek to acquire high-performance, low-latency AI compute hardware and provide access under long-term lease arrangements, meeting customer demand that spot markets and hyperscalers are unable to reliably service.”

Understated quote of the year so far: AI infrastructure expert Bill Kleyman called it a “strange pivot.”

“I’ve been in this industry a while, and a company like Allbirds moving from shoes into AI infrastructure is not a very natural adjacency.”

If androids dream of electric sheep, perhaps wool-based AI has a future.

And in the Wall Street Research Corne, Goldman Sachs has rebalanced its Long Duration Basket following the recent rise in rates.

These are stocks whose expected cash flows sit further out in the future, making them more sensitive to interest rate moves. The basket is sector-neutral relative to the Russell 1000 (IWB).

Names in the group include Tesla (TSLA), DraftKings (DKNG), Costco (COST), AbbVie (ABBV), Snowflake (SNOW) and Broadcom (AVGO).

See the whole list in our story.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: This Dividend Growth King Has Rarely Been So Cheap
data.description:
On Thursday morning, Abbott Labs (ABT) reported first quarter results, presenting investors with a modest revenue beat on the top-line and in-line earnings. However, the stock traded 4-5% lower in the pre-market as sellers were disappointed in earnings guidance. At issue were costs from the recent acquisition of Exact Sciences, with the $21B deal impacting full year profits by approximately $0.20/share over the course of 2026. The acquisition, which closed on March 23rd, has been taken as a negative by the market and is the primary reason shares are down over 20% so far in 2026.

Investors willing to look past acquisition-related weakness can find many optimistic factors in the Q1 report. Profits and sales grew by mid-single digits in Q1 (~6-7%), with steady growth seen for the remainder of 2026. While Abbott's $2B nutrition segment continues to underperform (-6% YoY), the acquisition presents TTM sales of $3.2B in the oncology diagnostics market, adding a new high-growth vertical equivalent to 7% of revenues. As medicine moves toward a personalized experience, particularly in oncology, this positions Abbott to capitalize on a major secular growth trend.

In addition, Abbott has rarely been this cheap. 2017 marked the 10-year nadir of valuation, with shares trading as low as 15.3x, however, the company has rarely traded below 20x. Presently, the midpoint of 2026 earnings guidance is $5.48/share, putting the price around 17.7x forward earnings, more or less the cheapest in 10 years. The company's price relative to sales and cash flow paints a similar picture, hinting that the market's dour sentiment may be close to bottoming.

Abbott Labs is also a Dividend King, having raised its annual dividend for 52 consecutive years. Shares yield 2.6% with 5-year annualized growth of 8.5%, offering a good starting yield with significant growth ahead. For dividend growth investors, Abbott's strong history, low valuation, secular growth, and de-risked uncertainty earn a Buy rating.

Abbott Laboratories: Q1 2026 Results

Abbott's Q1 performance was largely defined by the underlying strength of its core MedTech portfolio, which masked ongoing weakness in the company's nutrition segment. In Q1 2026, Abbott's sales by segment were 19% in nutrition (-6% YoY growth), 20% from diagnostics (6% YoY growth), 12% from established pharmaceuticals (13% YoY growth) and 48% from medical devices (13% YoY growth).

In total, this led Abbott to report revenue of $11.2B (7.8% YoY growth), edging past analyst estimates of $11.0B. Organic comparable sales grew at a robust 3.7%, while adjusted earnings hit Wall Street's expectations exactly at $1.15/share, representing 6% growth from the prior year.

Weakness in shares was attributable to the reduced EPS guidance for 2026 compared to previously released guidance. Now a mid-point of $5.48/share is expected, $0.20/share below the prior guidance.

Abbott Q1 2026 Results* Actual Consensus Outcome Difference
Revenue $11.16B $11.00B Beat $160M
Adjusted EPS $1.15 $1.15 In-Line 0
Comparable Sales Growth 3.70% -- -- --
Abbott Q2 Guidance $1.25-$1.31 $1.32 Below -$0.04 at mid-point
Abbott FY 2026 Guidance $5.38-$5.58 $5.47 Beat $0.01 at mid-point
Comparable Sales Growth 6.5-7.5% 6.87% Beat +0.13% at mid-point
Previous 2026 Guidance $5.55-$5.80 -- Below Mid-point Reduced by $0.20/share

*Data from Seeking Alpha and Abbott's Q1 Press Release.

To understand the market's negative reaction, and why it represents an opportunity, investors must look at management's philosophy. CEO Robert Ford is positioning Abbott for continued growth in oncology diagnostics. Since taking on the role of CEO in March 2020, Ford has chosen to make bold pivots rather than simply massaging quarter-to-quarter results to smooth the company's EPS trajectory.

The $0.20/share reduced EPS guidance for 2026 likely represents the peak of Wall Street's concern regarding costs from the Exact Sciences acquisition. Shares have been punished in 2026 because Wall Street hates aggressive spending to fuel growth at the expense of reliable quarterly beats. However, this is exactly what Abbott should be doing to position itself for growth in the coming years, even at the expense of short-term pain.

Exact Sciences premier product is Cologuard, an at-home DNA-based test to screen for colorectal cancer. Cologuard uses 10 DNA markers in a biomarker panel to find colon cancer even in its early stages when it is more treatable. This strategy should allow Abbott to remain a steady growth compounder in the years ahead.

Abbott Laboratories: Historical Valuations

Since peaking last June around $139/share, Abbott has fallen significantly and now trades around 30% below all-time highs. On the basis of a number of metrics the company is nearly as cheap as it has been over the past 10 years. Abbott trades at 23x FCF, 16x EV/EBITDA and 3.83x sales, providing investors with a compelling entry point, or a watch-list stock in advance of further signs that a technical bottom has formed.

Because the market is currently punishing the stock for the deal's price tag, Abbott has been pushed into rare valuation territory. Following the post-earnings selloff, shares are trading around $97, pushing the forward P/E ratio to 17.7x based on the mid-point of revised 2026 guidance. The current valuation has priced in integration risks, while ignoring the substantial growth ahead from Cologuard and a pipeline of new tests.

Abbott Laboratories: Analyst Projections

In 2026, Abbott's revenue growth will accelerate due to the Exact Sciences acquisition, with the company forecasting around 7% comparable sales growth and analysts seeing 14.2% sales growth, approximately 7% of which comes from the acquisition. As this moderates in later years, Abbott is expected to continue its top-line revenue growth at a CAGR of around 7%.

Due to significant operational leverage, this will translate into higher EPS growth in the next five years. While 2026 comes in below trend growth (6.3%) due to acquisition costs, once costs normalize, Wall Street sees a return of 9-12% EPS growth for the foreseeable future.

Looking forward, the broader analyst consensus recognizes the strategic rationale of the acquisition, even if Wall Street myopically hates relatively minor fluctuations in Abbott's EPS growth trend.

The critical inflection point will come as 2027 guidance approaches. Once the approximately $100M in cost synergies are realized for the merger, the earnings growth trend should stabilize. Investors buying now, around peak uncertainty of the long-term EPS trend, are positioned for the stock to return toward its historically normalized valuation.

Conclusion

For long-term investors, the current setup offers a compelling asymmetry. The market is hyper-focused on the fractional, frictional costs of a massive acquisition while overlooking the long-term benefits. This is leading to panic selling below $100/share, offering compelling prices due to a $0.20/share reduction in 2026 guidance that investors arguably should have already expected. Now with greater certainty over these costs, the stock should find a bottom in the coming months.

In the longer term, I am a believer in CEO Robert Ford's vision. Exact Sciences is about more than Cologuard. The company's pipeline contains tests for other cancers, such as breast, liver, and multi-cancer early detection. In the coming years, these should fuel growth, allowing Abbott to continue on as a Dividend King and extend its dividend growth streak to 53 years and beyond.

Abbott Laboratories is a Buy with a 12-month price target of $126/share, representing the midpoint P/E valuation seen in recent years (23x), applied to 2026 earnings guidance ($5.48/share).

data.title: Microsoft: Cheap For All The Wrong Reasons
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) has gone out of the market’s favor, and I don’t say this lightly. The stock is now the worst-performing in the Mag 7, as seen below.

Microsoft is up around 4% over the past month, underperforming everyone in the peer group, with the exception of Tesla (TSLA), which is flat. Over the 3-month, 6-month, 9-month, and year-to-date periods, the stock is in deep, double-digit red territory, all while nothing has changed fundamentally.

In my August 2025 coverage, I maintained the stock with a Buy at the $504 levels, mainly on Azure pulling in over $75 billion annually at 34% year-over-year growth. At the time, I thought that with over 65% of Fortune 500 companies already using the Azure OpenAI Service, and that Elon Musk's proposed Macrohard AI-native competitor, launched under xAI (X.AI), was unlikely to dent Microsoft's enterprise moat within any investable time horizon.

Displacing Windows, Office 365, Teams, and Azure from three decades of entrenched government, corporate, and education relationships was "practically impossible for any new entrant, at least within the next five to ten years." My thesis from back then still stands, but what’s different now is sentiment. That stock is now trading at $411, now down around 18% since my last coverage, and it even hit the low of $356 in late March. But the question arises: has anything materially changed in the underlying business to justify this steep reduction in market cap since that late July high at $552? The answer is no, and that is precisely why Microsoft is attractive at these levels.

What I think changed is the market's narrative around the stock, not the business itself. Last year, the market was rewarding Microsoft's AI capex as visionary, but by January of this year, that same capex had been reframed as a liability, as the stock fell 7% after the company spent $37.5 billion on capex for Q2, above consensus at $34.3 billion.

The market is now on the lookout for any deceleration in revenue growth amid the billions spent on data centers, especially in the cloud. So, while the underlying investments did not change, their interpretation did. And yet, the miss on cloud growth and guidance didn’t help these fears, with the company expecting 37% to 38% growth next quarter, in line with consensus at 37.1%, and reported revenue from Azure and other cloud services at 39%, below consensus at 39.4%, and below the 40% growth in the quarter prior.

Investment thesis

What I said in August about the competitive moat remains entirely intact. But the bull case in August assumed AI monetization would be visible and accelerating by now. According to the company’s CFO Amy Hood,

“RPU growth was again led by E5 and M365 Copilot, and paid M365 commercial seats grew 6% year-over-year to over 450 million with installed base expansion across all customer segments, though primarily in our small and medium business and frontline worker offerings.”

So, with 15 million paid seats, Microsoft 365 Copilot comes down to 3.3% penetration rate, for $30 per user a month. This is not an unusual pattern in enterprise software: the gap between active users and paying users at scale always takes longer to close than the initial adoption curve suggests, and it has extended the timeline for the ARPU expansion thesis to show up meaningfully in revenue.

The valuation situation today is very different from that of August. When I covered the stock at $504, it was trading at an 11.69x EV/Sales, a number I said was priced for near-perfection. Today, the stock is trading at 9.4x forward EV/Sales, materially lower, and more comparable with that of its peers.

I’m a buyer here, and on any weakness toward $360.

What the valuation table makes clear is that the market is not just punishing Microsoft, it is actively mispricing it relative to peers. On a forward GAAP P/E basis, MSFT at 24.01x is the cheapest name in the entire Mag 7, sitting below Meta (META), Nvidia (NVDA), Google (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Apple (AAPL). On forward EV/EBITDA, MSFT at 15.65x is second only to Amazon. The forward PEG ratio at 1.70x sits comfortably in line with the peer group, but still below Google and Amazon.

The stock is also underperforming the cloud peer group on the 1-year and the 3-year mark, up only 42% on the latter, while Google is up 214% and Amazon another 141.8%. Microsoft, at these levels, wins on growth-adjusted valuation, and it's not even close against Amazon and Google.

The stock has the highest revenue growth of the group on a trailing three-year CAGR basis at 14.39% versus 11.73% for Amazon and 12.51% for Google, and the strongest EBITDA growth year over year at 24.53%. The stock is currently trading at $411, and is set to open at $417, up 1.5% in pre-market trading on Thursday and building on the 4.6% jump on Wednesday.

The stock broke its EMA50 yesterday at $398, and is now on the way to the EMA200 at $441, around a 6.5% gain away. While the technical picture doesn’t look the healthiest, it does look hopeful, with RSI recovering to 42 from the low of 21 back in late March. Both EMA21 and EMA50 are trending upwards, though they remain bearishly aligned at this point, with EMA200 above its short-term moving averages.

I’m a buyer here, and on any weakness toward $360.

The leaked memo:

OpenAI (OPENAI) sent a memo to its staff on Sunday, and it didn’t exactly favor Microsoft. The company’s new revenue chief, Denise Dresser, addressed the partnership with Amazon on the memo, which is now gaining favourability, after the latter invested up to $50 billion in OpenAI, as opposed to Microsoft’s $13 billion since 2019, back in the early innings.

“Our Microsoft partnership has been foundational to our success. But it has also limited our ability to meet enterprises where they are — for many that’s Bedrock…Since we announced the partnership at the end of February, inbound demand from our customers for this offering has been frankly staggering.”

The competition between the AI giants OpenAI and Anthropic (ANTHRO) is intensifying, especially after Anthropic revealed a run-rate revenue surpassing $30 billion, up from $9 billion from late last year. According to Dresser,

“They use accounting treatment that makes revenue look bigger than it is, including grossing up rev share with Amazon and Google…We report Microsoft rev share net, which is more inline with standards we would be held to as a public company,“

and went on to talk about Anthropic’s mistake of “not acquiring enough compute.” Needless to say, this memo landed like a grenade in the middle of the most sensitive debate in large-cap tech right now: the durability of Microsoft's OpenAI moat. Dresser is not announcing a breakup with Microsoft, nor is she signaling that OpenAI is migrating its core infrastructure off Azure. Instead, she is publicly legitimizing the Amazon-Bedrock relationship as a coequal distribution channel for OpenAI's enterprise ambitions, framing the Microsoft exclusivity not as a strategic strength but as a constraint that has limited OpenAI's ability to serve customers.

The narrative of Microsoft as OpenAI's indispensable financial go-to gets complicated when the counterparty's own revenue chief is publicly celebrating a competing investor's scale and reach. Markets will read this, and rightfully so, as a signal that OpenAI's dependency on Microsoft is declining as the AI race matures. Basically, if enterprise customers increasingly access OpenAI models through AWS Bedrock rather than Azure, the inference compute revenue that the AI acceleration thesis depends on goes to Amazon, not Microsoft. While I don’t think that’s a 2026 problem, it is a 2027 and 2028 problem that the market will begin pricing now.

What’s next:

The April 29 earnings call just became even more important. Nadella will be asked directly about the OpenAI-Amazon partnership and what it means for Azure's competitive positioning. His answer, and more importantly, the Azure Q3 growth and Q4 guidance, will either contain the damage from this memo or confirm that the concentration risk the market has been pricing since January is, in fact, a structural rather than a temporary concern. Copilot monetization will also be a point of focus, along with capex spend after a record $37.5 billion quarterly spend in Q2.

This is not the first time Microsoft has been written off, and history suggests it will not be the last. On one last note, Microsoft announced a collaboration with Stellantis (STLA) to “co-develop more than 100 AI initiatives across sales, customer care and operations, driving innovation at global scale.” According to the press release, these include

“AI‑powered product development and validation, Predictive maintenance and testing, Faster deployment of new digital features and services.”

Microsoft's Commercial Business CEO, Judson Althoff, said this

“reflects a shared ambition to drive AI Transformation responsibly and securely across the automotive value chain…By combining Stellantis’ global scale and engineering expertise with Microsoft’s trusted cloud, AI and security platforms, we are delivering real value for millions of drivers worldwide.”

Deals like the Stellantis collaboration rarely move the needle, or the stock, but they are data points in the broader enterprise AI adoption story that the market is currently discounting, and that's why they matter.

This is also a nice reminder that Microsoft's enterprise AI opportunity is not dependent on any single partner or product.

data.title: ETW: Discounted Opportunity With Monthly Pay
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The discount on the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETW) has remained wide since our last update. As a closed-end fund, discounts can remain wide for significant periods of time. In this case, it has been a number of years now since the fund was flirting with a premium level. That said, the discount still remains enticing at these levels, and on the back of added broader equity volatility, we can sometimes see that discount get even deeper.

ETW Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.00
  • Discount/Premium: -9.48%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.51%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.1%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.1 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

ETW "invests in a diversified portfolio of common stocks and writes call options on one or more U.S. and foreign indices on a substantial portion of the value of its common stock portfolio to seek to generate current earnings from the option premium."

The tax-managed focus comes in with the "fund evaluating returns on an after-tax basis and seeks to minimize and defer federal income taxes incurred by shareholders in connection with their investment in the Fund."

Performance - Deep Discount Remains Persistent

Since our last write-up, ETW has outperformed the S&P 500 Index. However, this has been quite a volatile period, where we entered into a correction and then rebounded sharply.

The discount level still remains relatively attractive, though off the double-digit discount level we had been seeing. Overall, I believe this still continues to represent a time to consider adding to this fund based on the discounted valuation.

Looking back at longer-term historical results, the fund provides a breakdown of various benchmarks. These all help to highlight the global nature of the fund, the equity focus, but also the call writing strategy.

Against the call writing indexes, the fund has outperformed or provided competitive results in all time frames. Against the vanilla indexes, it has been more of a struggle, but that isn't all that unexpected.

As is the case with all call writing funds, one of the drawbacks is potentially capping upside participation when the underlying index rallies above the strike price selected. ETW has historically targeted nearly a 100% overwrite level, but this has varied more recently. In our prior update, the fund was only overwritten by around 43%. Now, they are back up to 96% overwritten.

By being more flexible in the level at which they are overwritten, that can help to limit the potential upside cap that the fund would have historically run into. Given that they are back up to nearly 100% overwritten, I'm not sure if it was just a short-term experiment by the team for a quarter or two, or if this is something they will tool with more often going forward. So this is something I'll be trying to pay attention to more often.

Another way to help mitigate that upside cap is using a laddered approach by writing various call options on different indexes, different expiration dates and strike prices. While these particular trades have all expired now as of the last annual report, the below can still give us an idea of the overall approach more broadly. There were 16 other option trades listed on the next page of the annual report, which included most of their S&P 500 Index positions.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

The current distribution rate works out to 8.51%, with the NAV rate coming down to 7.71%. The market rate gets a nice boost relative to what the underlying portfolio has to generate thanks to the sizeable discount of the fund. Over time, the fund has made several adjustments to the monthly payout. Initially, the fund started with a quarterly distribution schedule as well. Most of the distributions have been reductions lower, which can be discouraging to some investors.

However, Eaton Vance has historically tried to keep their payout relatively more reasonable. When equities decline sharply for lengthy periods, and NAV rates start to push toward 10% or higher, they tend to cut the payout to attempt to preserve some NAV. I tend to be okay with funds that cut when they need to. It is similar to ETFs that often pay out variable rates each week, month, or quarter, paying out only what they are earning. In the case of CEFs, they tend to get more punished when they reduce their payout because discounts can widen—something that doesn't occur meaningfully in ETFs.

Overall, to fund the distribution, the payout will primarily require capital gains. This isn't that uncommon for any equity-focused CEF. Those capital gains can come from the underlying portfolio or from collecting the option premiums generated by its index call writing strategy.

To make the distribution tax-friendly, as the "tax-managed" portion of its name would suggest, it can do so by realizing losses either on its option writing overlay strategy or by realizing losses on underlying positions. In their last annual report, the fund listed nearly $52.5 million in realized losses from the options strategy, which would have offset some of the realized gains they produced from their portfolio.

In this way, the fund can generally "manage" the portfolio in a way to reduce tax obligations through some of the distribution being classified as return of capital. Alternatively, qualified dividends and long-term capital gain tax rates are also relatively lower than ordinary income. That all can make it appealing to hold in a taxable account for investors.

For ETW in 2025, the fund listed nearly all of its ordinary dividends as qualified (but unfortunately not 100%), with a small portion being nondividend distributions ("ROC") and then the bulk being long-term capital gains.

ROC distributions can be even more beneficial than LTCG because they are tax-deferred, as they reduce an investor's cost basis. Therefore, the tax obligation doesn't potentially come until the investor chooses to sell their position.

ETW's Portfolio

Given the strategy focus of the fund, which is to limit tax obligations, the portfolio turnover rate is rather low. Though this is also a function of the fund attempting to primarily replicate the underlying blended benchmarks that the fund also follows, which tend to see minimal movement over time. In the last year, the fund's turnover rate came in at 7% and has averaged over the last five years at only 6%.

The main takeaway here is that the portfolio does not tend to change too significantly when looking at the fund quarter-to-quarter. The broader geographic exposure comes in at ~55%—virtually the same as it was during our last update at the beginning of the year.

That is similar to the sector allocation, which tends to reflect the S&P 500 Index with its overweight tilt to information technology. That comprises almost 29% of the fund's exposure. Though they may mirror the allocation, they do so with around 263 holdings.

Given the overall strategy of the fund and the sector allocation listed above, it won't be too surprising to most investors that the top ten names include the mega-cap tech Magnificent 7 names primarily.

This was also the case in our prior update, with some minor percentage weighting fluctuations. Those are going to primarily be the result of natural shifts due to market price fluctuations from day-to-day. Along with several Mag 7 names, we do get to see some representation of its global exposure, so that can add a touch more diversification. The weighting of the Mag 7 names is also reduced relative to how the S&P 500 Index has them allocated.

Conclusion

ETW remains stubbornly at a rather deep discount that has been fairly persistent for a number of years now. I still believe that makes it a worthwhile candidate for adding at this time. While we've bounced off some of the lows in the broader equity market, ETW is still down a bit from its recent highs. That can add even more appeal to consider adding at this time, as some valuations from the underlying portfolio have come down from where they were.

data.title: The Growing Disconnect Between Wall Street And Main Street
data.description:
I sat down and watched 'The Big Short' for the first time in many years this week. The movie remains iconic in my view and by far the best flick made around the subprime crisis that triggered the Great Financial Crisis.

It still makes my blood boil that almost no one responsible for helping nearly bring the country into the financial dark ages has seen a day in jail more than 15 years later. Not the regulators, not the financial cheerleaders on CNBC, not the rating agencies, and only an inadequate number of mortgage brokers that gave a mortgage to anyone could breathe via NINJA loans and other creative financial hocus pocus.

Only one of the bankers in the middle of the whole thing ever saw the inside of a prison cell. Even those that created the likes of synthetic CDOs and other derivative weapons of mass destruction. And don't even get me started on the ratings agencies that routinely rubber-stamped a toxic brew of subprime mortgages and their tranches with AA ratings. Heck, the stock of Moody's (MCO) is up roughly thirty-fold from its nadir in early 2009.

Meanwhile, Main Street got to pay for that debacle with peak unemployment rates of 10%, millions of foreclosures and evictions, and crushed 401K balances, as well as trillions of lost home equity. Not to mention a massive surge in federal debt that technically belongs to U.S. taxpayers.

And this brings me to the topic of today's column: the once again growing disconnect between Wall Street and Main Street. This is one factor within what is becoming a more bifurcated U.S. economy. This economic trend has been widely dubbed the 'K-Shaped' economy. This environment is one where people in the upper arm of the 'k' see their wealth increase while those in the bottom half go in the other direction.

And you can see this everywhere, and this trend was exacerbated during the pandemic. The Federal Reserve boosted the M2 money supply by 40% over two years and kept interest rates effectively at zero for too long as the inflation surge this helped trigger was neither 'temporary' nor 'transitionary.' This helped inflation levels rise to their highest mark since the Stagflation of the late 1970s, with the CPI peaking at 9.1% in June of 2022.

As a result, most American households lost buying power against inflation from 2021 to 2023. Those at the top of the income scale largely were immune to this trend, as this increased money supply helped ignite large asset price appreciation in real estate, equities, and other assets that offset the rise in prices throughout the economy.

With that backdrop, let's look at the current divergence between Wall Street and Main Street. Thanks to a surge of trading and fee revenue, Wall Street's profits rose 30% to $65.1 billion. This resulted in record bonuses to Wall Street employees, the average being nearly a quarter of a million dollars.

Meanwhile, on Main Street, two-thirds of Americans were living paycheck to paycheck in 2025. The U.S. personal savings rate is currently a dismal 4%.

Trading revenues at JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) jumped 20% year-over-year in Q1 to $11.6 billion. At Goldman Sachs (GS), trading revenues increased 27% to a record $5.3 billion in the first quarter. Dealmaking-related fees rose 48% to $2.8 billion.

On Main Street, delinquency rates on student loans, credit card debt, and auto obligations continue to move up. While record bonuses are boosting morale on Wall Street, U.S. consumer sentiment just hit an all-time low this month as 12-month inflation expectations moved up a full 100 bps to 4.8%.

The divergence between Wall Street and Main Street and the increase in wealth inequality in general are playing out in intriguing ways throughout the economy. Despite a relatively low unemployment rate, equities at all-time highs, and generally good GDP growth over the past year, the percentage of Americans that believe the country is heading in the wrong direction is nearly 60%.

In addition, this divergence is a factor behind the growing local resistance to new AI data centers. According to Data Center Watch, at least $156 billion in data center projects were significantly delayed or canceled in 2025 due to this increasing opposition.

It is intriguing to me that markets are at all-time highs despite the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, the surge in fuel and commodity prices, rising inflation expectations, growing worries around private credit, and most of the consumer population struggling substantially.

That said, the top 10% of American households by income now drive roughly half of all consumer spending. As long as this key cohort stays healthy, the U.S. economy will likely at least muddle along. However, if this demographic experiences increased stress, all bets are off.

What could change the dynamics for the top 10%? A significant decline in equity or real estate markets could result in a reverse wealth effect and trigger a pullback in spending from this top echelon. Increasing layoffs in top-paying professions due to AI could be another factor. Food for thought on this increasing divergence from Wall Street and Main Street.

data.title: Parnassus Value Select ETF Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Facts

Ticker PRVS
Total Expense Ratio 1 0.59%
Inception Date 12/11/2024
Benchmark Russell 1000 Value Index
Asset Class U.S. large cap value
Objective Capital appreciation

The strategy seeks to achieve strong long-term returns through a high-conviction, highly concentrated portfolio of approximately 25 undervalued U.S. large-cap stocks that are poised to rise but are temporarily out of favor relative to their history or peers.

Key Takeaways

  • Parnassus Value Select ETF (NAV)(PRVS) returned -1.45% (NAV) for the quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index's 2.10%.
  • The portfolio's performance was primarily impacted by our lack of exposure to the Energy sector, along with our holdings in the Health Care and Financials sectors. Performance was helped by our holdings in the Communication Services, Industrials and Information Technology sectors.
  • Despite the increased volatility in the first quarter, we continue to maintain a quality-oriented portfolio with balanced exposure across traditional value and secular themes.
  • Markets have repeatedly rewarded investors who maintained a disciplined approach and long-term orientation through periods of uncertainty. We believe our quality-anchored portfolio will prove resilient through the current volatility.

Market Review

The Russell 1000 Value Index added to 2025's strong return with a modest gain in the first quarter, significantly outperforming its growth peer, as geopolitical uncertainty put downward pressure on multiple expansion. Equity markets reversed course in the first quarter of 2026, as early optimism surrounding the ongoing megatrend in artificial intelligence (AI) and easier monetary policy yielded to geopolitical shocks and tighter financial conditions. While the AI megatrend remained intact, fears of AI disruption caused sharp sell-offs among asset-light businesses such as software. The Federal Reserve held interest rates steady due to persistent inflation and economic uncertainty. The escalation of the U.S.-Iran war drove oil prices sharply higher and resulted in a shift to a risk-off market sentiment, which pressured economically sensitive stocks while buoying the Energy sector. During the quarter, the best-performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value Index were Energy, Industrials and Consumer Staples, while the Financials sector ended the quarter with sharp losses. The Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary and Health Care sectors finished modestly lower.

Performance

As of 03/31/2026 3 Mos. 1 Yr. Since 12/11/2024
Parnassus Value Select ETF - NAV -1.45 18.81 8.26
Parnassus Value Select ETF - Market Price -1.66 18.54 8.17
Russell 1000 Value 2.10 15.87 10.46

Performance data quoted represent past performance and are no guarantee of future returns. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted, and current performance information to the most recent month end is available on the Parnassus website (www.parnassus.com). Investment return and principal value will fluctuate, so an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original principal cost. Returns shown in the table do not reflect the deduction of taxes a shareholder may pay on fund distributions or redemption of shares. The Russell 1000 Value Index is an unmanaged index of common stocks, and it is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index figures do not take any expenses, fees or taxes into account, but mutual fund returns do. The estimated impact of individual stocks on the Fund's performance is provided by FactSet.

Quarterly Performance Review

Energy underweight impacted relative performance

The Fund returned -1.45% (NAV) for the quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index's 2.10%. The portfolio's underperformance was largely attributed to a lack of exposure to the Energy sector, which delivered strong returns for the quarter due to rising energy prices and heightened geopolitical uncertainty rather than a structural change in fundamentals. Our Health Care and Financials holdings also detracted. Our holdings in the Communication Services, Industrials and Information Technology sectors boosted relative returns, as investors moderated expectations for growth stocks and rotated into companies with resilient and more predictable earnings.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Applied Materials (AMAT), which supplies semiconductor manufacturing equipment, benefited from higher demand from foundry customers tied to AI infrastructure build-out, as hyperscalers increased spending to support accelerating compute needs.

Deere (DE) advanced on expectations for an agricultural cycle recovery and continued adoption of precision agriculture technologies, with investors viewing the agricultural equipment business as both durable and future proofed.

Verizon Communications (VZ) shares climbed as the telecommunications company benefited from a change in strategy under a new CEO to prioritize subscriber retention, while investors shifted into more defensive stocks over the period due to market volatility.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.'s (TSMC)(TSM) central role in AI chip production continued to bolster investor confidence and drive stock gains, as company management announced higher-than-anticipated capital expenditure guidance.

AstraZeneca (AZN), a multinational pharmaceutical and biotechnology company, outperformed as the company demonstrated strong pipeline execution, boosted by resilient demand across oncology and specialty medicines. The shift into more defensive names amid the market volatility also boosted investor sentiment.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

Microsoft (MSFT) shares ended lower amid continued investor fears around the potential return on its elevated AI-related capital expenditures, despite the company reporting healthy financial results.

CBRE Group (CBRE) shares declined on higher financing costs for the commercial real estate sector and fears that AI could disrupt the company's traditional commercial real estate brokerage business model. We believe AI will be net additive to CBRE's moat given its vast data advantage.

S&P Global (SPGI) underperformed as uncertainty around the theoretical ease of replacing financial data providers with AI tools weighed on sentiment. We expect the company to remain an important player in its debt rating, data and risk management market segments.

Visa (V) shares fell despite management's strong 2026 earnings outlook, as litigation-related overhangs, regulatory concerns and a more cautious macro backdrop weighed on investor sentiment. Still, we believe Visa is a net beneficiary of AI, with its exposure to agentic commerce.

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), a global provider of pharmaceutical research instruments and services, declined alongside life sciences peers as investors remained cautious on the pace of end-market recovery, despite fundamentals tracking expectations.

Portfolio Positioning

Maintaining a quality-oriented portfolio amid heightened uncertainty

We continue to maintain a quality-oriented portfolio with balanced exposure across traditional value and secular themes. While geopolitical risks have risen in recent months, valuations are also more attractive for companies with high earnings quality. The current environment offers opportunity for value investors with a quality focus and long-term investment horizon.

The Portfolio's largest sector overweights are in Information Technology, Health Care and Communication Services. Our largest underweights are in Energy, Consumer Staples and Industrials.

Within Information Technology, our portfolio weight increased modestly as our semiconductor holdings rose during the quarter. The AI investment cycle continues to broaden to a wider set of ecosystem players, benefiting our holdings such as Applied Materials, Micron and TSMC. We remain optimistic about the adoption of AI but believe selectivity through bottom-up stock picking remains critical because opportunities and risks are highly company specific and spread across different sectors and time horizons.

During the quarter, portfolio exposures decreased modestly within Health Care and increased modestly within Communications Services on account of stock price movements. Our defensive holdings in those sectors, such as Verizon and AstraZeneca, held up well during this period of market weakness. This exemplifies our balanced approach to portfolio construction, with ample focus on resilience during periods of market volatility.

Our underweight positioning in Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary sectors remained broadly unchanged over the quarter. We continue to maintain diversified risk-adjusted exposure to consumer spending through payment networks and bank card issuers.

We view the relatively resilient economic data and company fundamentals as an attractive risk/reward opportunity for investors today. We continue to maintain our highest convictions in undervalued quality companies with the financial strength to withstand economic headwinds and positioned to benefit from long-term thematic tailwinds.

Outlook

Disciplined value investing through volatility and uncertainty

Value stocks continued their outperformance during the first quarter. The Russell 1000 Value Index has now significantly outperformed its growth peer over the last six months. This renewed period of market uncertainty—driven by the conflict in the Middle East, concerns about disruption from AI and volatility in interest rates—continues to favor stocks with more reasonable valuations over those embedding high market expectations. We believe our quality-anchored portfolio should prove resilient if geopolitical risks broaden into bigger headwinds for the global economy. Moreover, fundamental tailwinds underpinning long-term upside in the companies we hold in the portfolio remain intact despite near-term market volatility.

Every crisis is different in some ways and carries with it a different set of risks. However, looking back on the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the Ukraine war in 2022, the regional banking crisis in 2023 and the tariff-driven sell-off of 2025, a consistent lesson emerges: Markets have repeatedly rewarded investors who maintained a disciplined approach and long-term orientation through periods of uncertainty. This lesson also applies to how we evaluate management teams. We invest behind leaders with demonstrated experience in navigating uncertainty and the ability to stay steadfast in driving long-term shareholder value.

As stewards of your capital, we remain vigilant about macro risks, but our fundamental, bottom-up investment process avoids undue reliance on investing around macro outcomes. In our experience, fundamental factors can far outweigh near-term macro factors in generating attractive returns. We invest with a long-term time horizon that helps us weather short-term volatility and stay focused on what matters most—owning quality businesses at attractive valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Glossary

Earnings Growth is the change in a company's reported net income over a period of time.

Allocation Effect: the allocation effect in performance attribution measures how much of a portfolio's outperformance or underperformance against a benchmark comes from the manager's decision to overweight or underweight specific sectors or asset classes, rather than from picking individual stocks within them.

Important Information

PIL-916805-2026-04-14

The Russell 1000® Value Index is a market capitalization weighted index that measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth rates. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years). The Russell 1000® Value Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large cap value segment. The index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect value characteristics.
